summary:
Dutch Formosa (1624–1662 AD) was the [[event:2357|VOC's]] colonial territory on the western coast of Taiwan — the first large-scale European colonial presence on the island, established at Fort Zeelandia on the Tainan coast as a base for trade with China and Japan. Taiwan at the time was sparsely populated by Austronesian Indigenous peoples (ancestors of modern Taiwan's aboriginal peoples); Dutch colonists introduced Han Chinese laborers and settlers from the Fujian coast to cultivate sugar and rice, initiating the demographic transformation that would make Taiwan predominantly Han Chinese. The Dutch administered their territory through a combination of direct governance, missionary activity (Protestant missions converted significant Indigenous populations and created written scripts for several Indigenous languages), and economic exploitation through the Company's trading monopolies.

Dutch Taiwan was crucial to the VOC's broader Asian trading network: Taiwan served as the entrepôt between Chinese and Japanese commerce, since the Dutch could buy Chinese goods in Taiwan (from smuggling Chinese merchants) and sell them to Japan, circumventing Tokugawa restrictions on direct foreign trade at other ports. The colony ended abruptly in 1662 when the Ming loyalist general [[event:3306|Koxinga]] (Zheng Chenggong), fleeing the Qing conquest of mainland China, besieged Fort Zeelandia for nine months before the Dutch garrison surrendered and evacuated. Koxinga established a Ming loyalist regime in Taiwan that would resist Qing authority for two decades before finally being conquered in 1683.
